verses.text: Jesus stepped into a boat, crossed over, and came to his hometown.
Suddenly, some men brought him a paralyzed man on a stretcher. Seeing their faith, Jesus said, “Take courage, son; your sins are forgiven.”
Some scribes said to themselves, “This man is blaspheming.”
Jesus knew their thoughts and said, “Why think evil in your hearts?”
Which is easier to say, ‘Your sins are forgiven,’ or to say, ‘Rise and walk’?
But so you know that the Son of Man has authority to forgive sins,” he said to the paralytic, “Rise, pick up your bed and go home.”
So he rose and went home.
When the crowds saw this, they were awestruck and praised God who had given such authority to humankind.
As Jesus was going away from there, he saw a man named Matthew at the tax booth. “Follow me!” he said. And Matthew stood up and followed him.
While Jesus was eating at the house, behold, many tax collectors and sinners were coming and eating with him and his disciples.
When the Pharisees saw this, they said to his disciples, “Why does your teacher eat with tax collectors and sinners?”
Jesus heard and said, “It is not the healthy who need a physician, but the sick.”
Go and learn what this means: ‘I desire mercy, not sacrifice.’ For I came not to call the righteous, but sinners.

sections.headline: The Old Cannot Bear the New

verses.text: Then John's disciples approached and asked, “Why do we and the Pharisees fast often, while your disciples do not?”
Jesus said, “Can wedding guests mourn while the bridegroom is with them? Days will come when he is taken from them, and then they will fast.
No one sews an unshrunk patch onto an old garment. If they do, the patch pulls away, leaving the tear worse than before.
Nor do they pour new wine into old wineskins. If they do, the skins burst, the wine spills, and both are ruined. New wine goes into fresh wineskins, and both are preserved.
While he was speaking, behold, a synagogue official came and knelt before him. “My daughter has just died,” he said. “But come, place your hand on her, and she will live.”
Jesus got up and followed him, and so did his disciples.
Suddenly, a woman who had suffered a hemorrhage for twelve years approached from behind and touched the edge of his cloak.
For she said to herself, “If I only touch his cloak, I will be saved.”
Jesus turned, saw her, and said, “Take courage, daughter. Your faith has saved you.” And the woman was healed from that hour.
When Jesus entered the official's house and saw the flute players and the uproar of mourning,
he said to them, “Go away, for the girl is not dead but asleep.” And they laughed at him.
When the crowd was sent out, he entered, took her hand, and the girl was raised up.
This news spread throughout all that region.
As Jesus went on from there, two blind men followed him, crying out, “Have mercy on us, Son of David!”
When he came into the house, the blind men came to him. Jesus said, “Do you believe I am able to do this?” They said, “Yes, Lord.”
Then he touched their eyes and said, “According to your faith, let it be done for you.”
Their eyes were opened, and Jesus sternly warned them, “See that no one knows.”
Yet they went out and spread the news about him throughout all that region.
Behold, as they were going away, they brought to him a demon-possessed man who was not able to speak.
When the demon was cast out, the mute man spoke. The crowds were amazed and said, “Nothing like this has ever happened in Israel!”
But the Pharisees said, “He casts out demons by the ruler of demons!”
Jesus was going around all the towns and the villages, teaching in their synagogues, proclaiming the good news of the kingdom, and healing every disease and every sickness.
When he saw the crowds, he felt compassion for them, because they were weary and scattered, like sheep without a shepherd.
Then he said to his disciples, “The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few.
Therefore, pray to the Lord of the harvest to send out workers into his harvest.”

footnotes.content: “stretcher” (Greek *phoreon* = portable bed/mattress; cf. 8:8, 9:6). “son” (Jesus uses *paidion*, often translated “child,” but context implies an adult male. “Son” retains affectionate tone).
“bed” (Greek kline = household bed; replaced “stretcher” post-healing for readability).

When Jesus quotes, “I desire mercy and not sacrifice,” He draws from Hosea 6:6 to confront the Pharisees' distorted understanding of righteousness. In its original context, this verse expresses YHWH's longing for covenantal loyalty—steadfast love expressed through compassionate action—rather than mere ritual compliance. Jesus applies it here to expose the emptiness of external religiosity that lacks mercy toward sinners. His mission is not to reward the self-righteous, but to call those aware of their need. The quotation reaffirms that God values a heart aligned with His mercy more than outward displays of piety, and it restores the prophetic call to love over legalism. The directive, “Go and learn what this means,” was a rabbinic challenge—an invitation to true understanding, not mere familiarity with Scripture.

The “bridegroom” metaphor portrays Jesus as the long-awaited Christ and covenant redeemer, drawing on Old Testament imagery where God is depicted as Israel's husband (cf. Isaiah 54:5; Hosea 2:16–20). His presence marks a time of joy and celebration, not mourning. Just as wedding guests do not fast while the bridegroom is present, so Jesus' disciples rejoice in his presence. But the phrase “the bridegroom will be taken away” foreshadows his death—when sorrow and fasting will be appropriate. The metaphor emphasizes the newness, intimacy, and temporary nature of Jesus' earthly ministry.

The patch metaphor emphasizes the futility of combining Jesus' new way with the old system of religious observance. An unshrunk patch sewn onto an old garment will pull away when washed, worsening the tear. Likewise, trying to attach Jesus' message of renewal to the brittle framework of legalism results in damage, not repair. The metaphor warns that the kingdom of God cannot be treated as a supplement to old covenant rituals—it demands a complete reweaving of the fabric of spiritual life.

The image of new wine in old wineskins illustrates the incompatibility between Jesus' kingdom message and the old religious structures. New wine, still fermenting, would burst brittle old skins—just as the dynamic, Spirit-filled life of the kingdom cannot be contained by rigid traditions or legalistic systems. The “fresh wineskins” represent a renewed vessel—new hearts, new covenant, new community—capable of bearing the transformative power of Jesus' teaching without rupture. The metaphor affirms that Jesus did not come to patch up old religion, but to inaugurate something entirely new.

The title “Son of David” is a messianic designation drawn from God's covenant with David in 2 Samuel 7:12–16, where He promises that David's throne will be established forever. By the time of Jesus, this phrase had become a widespread hope for a coming deliverer—a kingly figure who would restore Israel. When the blind men cry out, “Son of David,” they are not merely recognizing Jesus' lineage, but proclaiming Him as the long-awaited Messiah. Their plea reveals both theological insight and personal desperation: though physically blind, they perceive Jesus more clearly than many who see. This moment highlights how faith often resides not in physical sight, but in spiritual recognition of who Jesus truly is.
“weary and scattered” (Greek estenogrigoremēnoi kai errimmēnoi; metaphor from Numbers 27:17; leadership crisis in Israel).

The verb “pray” translates Greek erōtan, meaning “to ask” or “to petition,” often emphasizing earnest, specific requests (cf. Luke 11:9–13, where it underscores persistence in seeking divine provision). The metaphor of the “harvest” evokes Old Testament imagery (e.g., Joel 3:13; Revelation 14:14–20) of God’s eschatological gathering of people into His Kingdom, while “workers” highlights those commissioned to proclaim the Gospel and shepherd others. Jesus’ command reflects both the urgency of mission and reliance on divine initiative to raise laborers for spiritual harvest. A parallel request in Luke 10:2 precedes the sending of the seventy-two, linking prayer to active participation in God’s redemptive work (cf. also John 4:35–38).
